data.title: F.N.B. projects $1.495B-$1.535B 2026 net interest income while assuming no Fed cuts
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: F.N.B. Corporation (FNB) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "F.N.B. produced a solid quarter with net income of $137 million" and "EPS increased 19% over the first quarter of 2025 to $0.38," said (Chairman, President & CEO Vincent J. Delie), adding that "period-end loan growth of 3.9% annualized linked quarter was driven by core middle market C&I" and that growth "has not benefited from NDFI or lending into private credit, a category that we continue to avoid."
  • "We recently announced an 8% increase to our quarterly cash dividend to $0.13 per share" and "an additional $250 million for the repurchase of our common stock on top of the $50 million remaining," said (CEO Delie), also noting "$35 million repurchased in the first quarter of 2026" and that "F.N.B. has returned a total of $2.4 billion in capital to shareholders through both dividends and repurchases since 2009."
  • "We were thrilled to recently announce our partnership as the official and exclusive retail bank and financial provider to the Pennsylvania State University," said (CEO Delie), adding, "Beginning in July, Penn State's 90,000 students, faculty and staff will have exclusive access to F.N.B.'s on-campus banking services, including our proprietary eStore" and that F.N.B. "was also selected as the primary treasury management provider to all Penn State campuses."
  • "This month, our first ATM that offers foreign currency disbursement for Canadian dollars and Mexican pesos opened at the new Pittsburgh International Airport," said (CEO Delie), calling it "very rare across the banking industry."
  • "First quarter net income totaled $137 million or $0.38 per share, with total revenues up a strong 9.4% from a year ago period," said (Chief Financial Officer Vincent J. Calabrese), and added that "first quarter's net interest margin was 3.25%, down 3 basis points sequentially" and "the margin for the month of March was at 3.30%."

Outlook

  • "We are maintaining our full year balance sheet guidance for spot balances, projecting period-end loans and deposits to grow mid-single digits on a full year basis," said (CFO Calabrese).
  • "Full year net interest income is still expected to be between $1.495 billion and $1.535 billion," said (CFO Calabrese), while changing the rate assumption: "We are assuming no Fed interest rate cuts for 2026 versus our previous expectation for 2 25 basis point cuts."
  • "Second quarter net interest income is projected between $370 million and $380 million," said (CFO Calabrese), and "second quarter levels" of noninterest income are expected "between $90 million and $95 million."
  • "The full year guidance range for noninterest expense remains unchanged between $1 billion and $1.02 billion, but we now expect to be at the higher end of that range due to increased investments in franchise growth and new strategic initiatives," said (CFO Calabrese).

Financial results

  • "Loan activity began to accelerate late in the quarter" and "spot total loans and leases" ended at "$35.1 billion," said (CFO Calabrese), with "spot total deposits" at "$38.9 billion" and "noninterest-bearing deposits" up "$89 million."
  • "Noninterest income totaled $91 million," said (CFO Calabrese), including "capital markets income" of "$6.8 million" and "Wealth Management revenues" of "$21.8 million."
  • "Noninterest expense totaled $257.9 million," said (CFO Calabrese), and "other noninterest expense increased $6.8 million or 30% due to a combination of higher fraud losses, litigation-related expenses, and the impact of our mortgage down payment assistance program."
  • "The first quarter efficiency ratio remained solid at 56.1%," said (CFO Calabrese), and capital levels were framed as "the TCE ratio of nearly 9% and the CET1 ratio of 11.4%."

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked about C&I acceleration and pipelines; (Chief Credit Officer Gary L. Guerrieri) said, "The pipelines have increased significantly and are pretty close to near record levels" and cited "high-quality opportunities" and "some M&A activity."
  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked what drove higher-end expense guidance; (CFO Calabrese) said, "There's a variety," including "Clicks-to-Bricks," "30 de novos over the next 5 years," and investments to "create a 360 view of our customers."
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: Asked about NIM and deposit costs; (CFO Calabrese) said the March exit margin was "3.30%" and that guidance "implies that going up gradually a few basis points or so a quarter" as deposit seasonality and reinvestment/roll-offs evolve.
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: Asked about capital posture and Basel III; (CFO Calabrese) said, "we continue to see buybacks attractive at current valuation levels," and on Basel III, "it has a meaningful impact if it gets in place the way it is" and "that's not baked into our plans here."
  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: Pressed on deposit pricing pressure; (CFO Calabrese) said "there's still opportunity for that cost of deposits to come down" while (CEO Delie) said the key lever is "increasing the noninterest-bearing component in the mix."
  • David Smith, Truist: Asked about keeping mid-single-digit loan growth without expected cuts; (CEO Delie) pointed to pipelines, saying commercial pipelines "are up 10%" and that consumer pipelines were "nearly at an all-time high."
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Asked about M&A appetite; (CEO Delie) said, "we're going to be opportunistic" and "We're not actively in the market."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on funding and margin sustainability, including "deposit pricing pressure" (Gunther) and "NIM outlook" (Haire).
  • Management tone slightly positive in prepared remarks, emphasizing capital return and growth, including (CEO Delie) "We recently announced an 8% increase" and (CFO Calabrese) "We continue to expect strong positive operating leverage."
  • Compared with last quarter, the guidance language shifted on rates from (CFO Calabrese, Q4) "Our guidance assumes 2 25 basis point rate cuts" to (CFO Calabrese, Q1) "We are assuming no Fed interest rate cuts for 2026," while maintaining the net interest income range.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Capital return actions were newly emphasized in Q1, including (CEO Delie) "an additional $250 million" buyback authorization and "an 8% increase" in the dividend; in Q4, (CFO Calabrese) said for buybacks, "for 2026, I would expect we'd be at the same level or higher" and dividend discussions were described as "on the table."
  • Loan growth commentary moved from Q4 balance-sheet “reset” to Q1 acceleration, with (CEO Delie, Q4) "we focused on resetting the balance sheet" and (CFO Calabrese, Q1) "Loan activity began to accelerate late in the quarter."
  • Expense messaging shifted from Q4 expectations of cost takeouts to Q1’s near-term reinvestment, with (CFO Calabrese, Q1) expecting to be "at the higher end" of the expense range due to "new strategic initiatives."

Risks and concerns

  • "Delinquency, along with NPLs and OREO increased slightly," said (Chief Credit Officer Guerrieri), while "criticized loans increased slightly, consistent with the seasonality" and provision expense was "$19.4 million" to support growth.
  • "While we have not experienced any impact related to tariffs, we are maintaining the related qualitative overlays from a year ago due to the ongoing conflict and uncertainty in the Middle East," said (CCO Guerrieri), adding that the company is "monitoring the situation" closely.
  • Expense headwinds included "higher fraud losses" and "litigation-related expenses," per (CFO Calabrese), with mitigation framed as "disciplined" expense management and investments intended to "extract costs as we move forward."

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q1 2026 as a quarter of accelerating loan activity and stable credit metrics alongside a step-up in capital return, highlighted by a dividend increase, expanded buyback authorization, and maintained full-year guidance ranges despite changing the interest-rate outlook to no Fed cuts in 2026, while signaling higher-end expense expectations tied to technology, data, and growth initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Autoliv reiterates 2026 adjusted operating margin of 10.5% to 11% and $1.2B operating cash flow, while flagging $90M raw material headwind
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Autoliv, Inc. (ALV) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "The first quarter exceeded our expectations, driven by strong sales in March" (President, CEO Mikael Bratt), alongside "solid productivity improvements" and "reduced call-off volatility" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "Our positive trend in Asia continued with strong growth in India, South Korea and China" (President, CEO Bratt). He added, "In China, we continued to grow faster than light vehicle production especially with the Chinese OEMs, outperforming by more than 40 percentage points" (President, CEO Bratt), and said, "In India, we grew sales by 38% organically" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "Buybacks were paused as the company was in a restricted period following multiple filings and the announcement of a new CFO" (President, CEO Bratt). He reiterated that "Our USD 2.5 billion share repurchase authorization through 2029 remains unchanged" and stated "the ambition annual share repurchase between USD 300 million and USD 500 million" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "Hostilities in the Persian Gulf had a limited impact this quarter" (President, CEO Bratt), and he said, "Based on what we know today, we reiterate our full year 2026 guidance of flat organic sales" and "an adjusted operating margin of around 10.5% to 11%" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "I am also pleased that we introduced our first air bag for motorcycles as well as our first complete wearable airbag solution" (President, CEO Bratt), describing it as "building on our long-term strategy of growing outside our traditional core business" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "Our net sales were almost $2.8 billion, representing a 7% increase" and "The adjusted operating income decreased from USD 255 million to USD 245 million" (Executive VP of Finance & CFO Monika Grama). She also said, "During the quarter, we recovered approximately 70% of our U.S. tariff costs" (CFO Grama).

Outlook

  • "We expect to outperform light vehicle production by around 1 percentage points" and reiterated "Our organic sales is expected to be flat" (President, CEO Bratt), with guidance assumptions including "no material changes to tariffs or trade restrictions that are in effect as of April 10, 2026" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "For the full year 2026, our current assessment is for around USD 90 million gross impact from higher raw material pricing compared the previous assessment of around $30 million a quarter ago" (President, CEO Bratt). He added, "customer compensation mechanisms are in place and are expected to offset a meaningful portion of the cost impact, although there is typically a timing delay" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "Operating cash flow is expected to be around USD 1.2 billion" and "We expect CapEx to be below 5% of sales" (President, CEO Bratt), while also stating "we expect a tax rate of around 28%" (President, CEO Bratt).

Financial results

  • "Our consolidated net sales were almost USD 2.8 billion, the highest for a first quarter yet" (President, CEO Bratt). He said the increase was "mainly driven by USD 154 million positive currency translation effect and USD 14 million from higher tariff-related compensation" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "The adjusted operating margin was 8.9%, 1 percentage points lower than in the same quarter last year" (President, CEO Bratt). CFO Grama said, "The reported operating income of USD 237 million was USD 8 million lower mainly due to capacity alignment activities" (CFO Grama).

  • "Operating cash flow was a negative USD 76 million" (President, CEO Bratt). CFO Grama attributed the year-over-year change primarily to "a negative working effect of USD 349 million" and said the working-capital impact was "mainly due to temporary impact" (CFO Grama).

  • "The combination of unrecovered tariffs and the dilutive effect of the recovered portion resulted in a negative impact of around 40 basis points on our operating margin in the quarter" (CFO Grama), and she said, "We expect, though, most of the outstanding tariffs to be recovered later in the year" (CFO Grama).

Q&A

  • Gautam Narayan, RBC: asked whether mix between domestic vs. nondomestic OEMs in China benefited margins and how sustainable it is; President, CEO Bratt responded, "we don't disclose a breakdown of our earnings profile for customers or regions" and said, "we have focused on our Chinese OEMs as they are growing in their share of the total market" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Gautam Narayan, RBC: asked about Section 232 and USMCA exemptions; President, CEO Bratt said, "for us, it's mainly the USMCA structure that is relevant and that we have no changes at this point" and that recent rule changes were "a minor part of our total exposure and not meaningful" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Colin Langan, Wells Fargo: questioned why Autoliv’s planning assumption differs from S&P’s 2026 LVP outlook and asked about downside sensitivity; President, CEO Bratt said changes are "within the... margin of error" and added, "we have a high degree of flexibility in our system" while citing "normal decrementals... between 20% and 30% leverage" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Colin Langan, Wells Fargo: asked about drivers of higher raw-material costs and potential nylon shortages; President, CEO Bratt said "it's really the oil price that is the main driver" and added on supply, "we don't really see at this point any main concerns" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Mattias Holmberg, DNB Carnegie: asked why Q1 outperformance doesn’t carry through full-year guidance and about phasing/recovery of the $90 million raw-material headwind; President, CEO Bratt cited "mix development throughout the year" and said the net effect "is included in our guidance" while adding, "we will have a majority through the price mechanisms" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Hampus Engellau, Handelsbanken: asked if call-off stability is sustainable and about China launch timing; President, CEO Bratt said "the call-off stability was around 95%" and "we still believe that it should continue to improve under normal circumstances" (President, CEO Bratt). CFO Grama added on launches, "I wouldn't say that we have any surprises" (CFO Grama).

  • Emmanuel Rosner, Wolfe: asked for mix assumptions and margin expansion drivers; President, CEO Bratt said full-year planning assumes "a neutral mix" (President, CEO Bratt). CFO Grama said the company plans to offset "negative impact from raw materials and from inflationary impact on SG&A and RD&E" with "structural cost savings" and "customer compensations" (CFO Grama).

  • Jose Asumendi, JPMorgan: asked about benefiting from Chinese OEMs in Europe and working capital; President, CEO Bratt said "we are present in a broad base" of Chinese OEM platforms and referenced "2 exceptions" with captive solutions (President, CEO Bratt), while adding, "we won the first tender that was issued in Europe by Chinese OEM" (President, CEO Bratt). CFO Grama said full-year expectations imply "normalizing the working capital assumptions" (CFO Grama).

  • Jairam Nathan, Daiwa: asked whether the motorcycle airbag changes long-term growth assumptions and about buybacks; President, CEO Bratt said, "it doesn't really change the expectation" and that Mobility Safety Solutions contribution becomes meaningful "2030 before it becomes meaningful" (President, CEO Bratt), and added on repurchases, "We remain committed" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Björn Enarson, Danske Bank: asked about neutral mix assumptions vs. prior-year headwinds and Middle East exposure; President, CEO Bratt said, "we had some headwind last year" and "We are not expecting that to be reversed this year" (President, CEO Bratt), and added Middle East exposure is "very limited" (President, CEO Bratt).

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to skeptical, with repeated probing on sustainability of China outperformance, tariff rule changes, raw-material spikes, and downside scenarios, including "if the strait doesn't open, there's more downside" (Colin Langan, Wells Fargo).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded in Q&A, emphasizing limits on disclosure ("we don't disclose a breakdown") and uncertainty monitoring ("we follow this") while reiterating guidance (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Versus the prior quarter, more of the dialogue centered on geopolitical-driven LVP and commodities ("hostilities around the Persian Gulf") and less on year-end record performance framing; management maintained a similar confidence posture but with more caveats tied to evolving conditions (President, CEO Bratt).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The company highlighted a CEO-led CFO transition: Q4 2025 CFO was Fredrik Westin, while Q1 2026 introduced "our new CFO, Monika Grama" (President, CEO Bratt), and Q1 noted buybacks were paused during the restricted period (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Raw-material framing shifted from Q4’s expectation of "more around $30 million headwinds" for 2026 (Fredrik Westin) to Q1’s "around USD 90 million gross impact" for 2026 (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Tariff recovery commentary shifted from Q4’s "close to 100%" recovery in the quarter (President, CEO Bratt) to Q1’s "recovered approximately 70%" and references to delays from a new program (CFO Grama).

  • Q4 emphasized record full-year outcomes and a "clear path towards achieving our 10% adjusted operating margin target" (President, CEO Bratt), while Q1 closing remarks emphasized a "clear path towards achieving our 12% adjusted operating margin target" (President, CEO Bratt).

Risks and concerns

  • "Heightened geopolitical uncertainty from the hostilities around the Persian Gulf adds risk to energy markets, consumer confidence and overall industry volumes" (President, CEO Bratt), with mitigation described as scenario monitoring and readiness to adjust (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Raw-material inflation risk was tied to oil-linked inputs, with management citing possible impact to "textiles and plastics" and indirect exposure to "aluminum, helium and steel" (President, CEO Bratt), and mitigation via "productivity and cost reduction initiatives" plus customer compensation mechanisms (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Execution risks discussed included tariff recovery timing (CFO Grama) and working-capital volatility from end-of-quarter sales, which management said was temporary and expected to reverse (President, CEO Bratt; CFO Grama).

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as ahead of internal expectations on sales and operations, with continued momentum in Asia driven by China and India outperformance, while keeping full-year 2026 guidance unchanged for flat organic sales, a 10.5% to 11% adjusted operating margin, and about $1.2 billion of operating cash flow. The discussion repeatedly returned to investor-sensitive swing factors—tariff recovery timing, the step-up in the gross raw-material headwind to about $90 million, and geopolitical risks around the Persian Gulf—while management pointed to structural cost savings, productivity initiatives, and customer compensation mechanisms as the main offsets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Corporate America’s big job cuts in 2026 - What do we know so far?
data.description:
Corporate workers in America have faced the brunt of job cuts since the start of the year. If 2025 was a year of unprecedented layoffs, then the first four months of this year did nothing but heighten the distress for white-collar professionals.

With increasing pressure to cut down costs and restructure around artificial intelligence, U.S. companies are announcing layoffs left, right, and centre. According to data from Challenger, Gray & Christmas, March alone saw 60,620 job cuts, up 25% from 48,307 cuts announced in February. There were above 70K layoffs in the tech sector alone this year, as per website Layoffs.fyi.

WHAT TO EXPECT THIS YEAR

Even though the first quarter of the year recorded a 56% decline in job cuts, the prediction for the year is still alarming.

A survey by Resume.org found that 48% of companies are planning layoffs in 2026, with 17% going for job cuts “definitely” and 31% “probably” going for job cuts in Q1. Another 8% expect layoffs later in 2026.

More than 100 companies are planning to cut jobs in 2026, according to WARN Tracker's website. Some cuts are part of previously announced reductions. WARN Tracker is a website that tracks when companies file WARN notices ahead of laying off employees. The Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act of 1988 requires employers performing large layoffs or office closures to give at least 60 days' notice to employees and state officials ahead of mass layoffs or plant closures.

As per the website, over 20 companies filed WARN notices in April. Chipmaker Qualcomm (QCOM) recently laid off more than 60 workers, across California. Laid-off employees are anticipated to leave the company May 26, the website shows.

According to the WARN tracker, JPMorgan (JPM) and Bank of America (BOFA) are among the financial firms that filed notices. JPM is letting go 50-100 employees in California, effective May 21, while BofA will eliminate 100-250 employees in New York, effective June 24. Gilead Sciences (GILD) is also implementing layoffs in California, effective May 29, where it will let go of 50-100 employees.

But the situation is not entirely bleak. Data from Challenger shows that hiring plans rose 149% in March compared to a year earlier.

THE NUMBERS SO FAR

 

 

Here are some of the U.S. companies making headlines for job cuts so far in 2026:

Snap (SNAP)

Snap said it will cut about 1,000 jobs, around 16% of its workforce, and close 300+ open roles.
Disney (DIS)

Disney is preparing to eliminate about 1,000 positions, primarily targeting the company’s marketing department, according to a Wall Street Journal report.
Meta Platforms (META) Meta is planning to initiate widespread layoffs that could impact 20% or more of the company’s workforce, as per a Reuters report. The company also reportedly plans to cut "a few hundred" jobs that will affect staffers in Reality Labs.
Amazon.com (AMZN) Amazon in late January said it plans to cut about 16,000 corporate jobs. This reduction in staff comes after the tech giant slashed about 14,000 white-collar jobs in October 2025.
ASML (ASML) ASML in January said plans to cut about 1,700 jobs, mainly across its technology and IT units.
Oracle (ORCL) Oracle started laying off employees in March, with data suggesting the company may slash 20,000 to 30,000 positions, possibly cutting 12–18% of its global workforce.
Block (XYZ) Block slashed its staff by 40%  attributing the drastic change to AI.
United Parcel Service (UPS) UPS announced plans to eliminate an additional 30,000 jobs this year.
Pinterest (PINS) Pinterest in January said it would trim its workforce by less than 15% and shutter some offices.
Peloton (PTON) 

PTON announced plans to cut about 11% of its workforce, according to Bloomberg.
Citigroup (C)

Citigroup announced plans to eliminate about 1,000 jobs. 
Morgan Stanley (MS)  MS is reportedly eliminating roughly 3% of its global workforce, or about 2,500 jobs across all divisions.
Home Depot (HD)  HD chopped 800 jobs, primarily in its Atlanta store support center.
Atlassian (TEAM) The company announced it will reduce its global headcount by about 10%, or 1,600 roles.
Nike (NKE)  Nike announced plans to cut 775 jobs at the company's distribution centers.
Target (TGT)  Target in February, eliminated 500 jobs in its distribution centers and regional offices.
Autodesk (ADSK)

ADSK disclosed that it will be letting go about 7%, or roughly 1,000 of its employees worldwide as part of a restructuring.
Dow (DOW) 

In January said it will slash 4,500 jobs, or ‌13% of its total workforce.
BlackRock (BLK) 

Reportedly reduced global workforce by about 1%, or roughly 250 employees.
Vimeo (VMEO) 

Initiated global layoffs, saying the cutbacks will impact a “large portion” of the company’s total workforce. 
The Washington Post 

Executed deep layoffs, cutting one-third of staff and major newsroom sections to stem steep losses.

REASONS DRIVING THE CUTS

Many companies like Pinterest and Amazon pointed to AI as the factor behind for eliminating swaths of their workforces at once. Many analysts believe that the cuts are happening to shift capital for AI investments.

However, some critics are not convinced and argue that some firms are overstating AI’s role, a practice increasingly dubbed “AI-washing.” A study by the Yale Budget Lab found more than 700,000 tech layoffs since 2022 largely reflect a pullback from pandemic-era overhiring. Some analysts noted that framing layoffs around AI can be reassuring to investors and better than admitting planning errors or slowing demand.

data.title: Sony and Honda plan to keep their joint venture going even after the EV plans were canceled
data.description:
Sony Group (SONY) and Honda Motor (HMC) plan to continue to collaborate even though the 50-50 joint mobility venture has been called off, according to Nikkei.

Sony-Honda Mobility still has 400 employees on the payroll, although some of them are expected to be absorbed by the parent companies.

The joint venture was established in 2022 with a mission to evolve mobility from a mere means of transportation into new experiences.  Sony Group (SONY) and Honda Motor (HMC) hoped the JV could be used to build and sell electric vehicles. The joint venture combined Sony's (SONY) strengths in imaging, sensing, software, and entertainment with Honda's (HMC) automotive engineering and manufacturing expertise.

The idea took shape after Sony (SONY) showed off its Vision-S concept cars in 2020 and 2022, which signaled its interest in entering mobility. In March 2022, the two companies signed a memorandum of understanding, and by June they announced the new venture would be called Sony Honda Mobility Inc.

data.title: 'Project Hail Mary' to extend theater run, delaying streaming payday
data.description:
Amazon MGM’s (AMZN) blockbuster hit about a science-teacher-turned-astronaut will be extending its run in movie theaters to give moviegoers an opportunity to enjoy “a movie that needs to be seen on a big screen.”

“We announced yesterday that MGM is extending the exclusive theatrical window for ‘Project Hail Mary,’ so it won’t be on streaming anytime soon,” said the movie’s co-director Christopher Miller at Las Vegas CinemaCon.

Project Hail Mary, which has already made over half a billion dollars during its one-month run—overtaking former box-office champs Gravity ($364M), The Martian ($374M), and Interstellar ($450M)—is the top-grossing film of 2026. The movie will run at IMAX (IMAX) theaters for a limited run.

While the trend has typically been to release movies to streaming after a short run at the theaters—sometimes as short as 30 days—the decision to hold Project Hail Mary longer, along with recent comments from Paramount Skydance (PSKY) CEO David Ellison for a 45-day run, suggests streaming channels may have to wait before taking advantage of a film’s popularity. 

Streaming channels count on movie releases for new subscribers and to reduce churn, sometimes for as long as four months. But some—like Amazon Prime, YouTube, and AppleTV—can also earn direct revenue from movie releases through rental and purchase fees. These fees can run as much as $20 per film per subscriber and can generate a meaningful source of revenue for the streaming service.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: NVDA, VZ, MRVL, HIMS
data.description:
Recent analyst activity includes bullish shifts for Hims & Hers (HIMS) and NVIDIA (NVDA), with analysts highlighting regulatory potential and massive execution in the AI space as key drivers for these upgrades. On the downgrade side, Marvell (MRVL) and Verizon (VZ) have seen rating cuts reflecting concerns over stretched valuations following rallies and emerging long-term disruption risks from competitors like Amazon.

Upgrades

  • Hims & Hers Health (HIMS): Upgrade to Buy by Petri Dish Reports. The analyst points to a critical regulatory junction regarding compounded GLP-1 therapies that could significantly validate the company’s business model and reverse its current valuation discount.

    “I feel Hims is being valued less like a mature healthcare company and more like a hybrid consumer-tech platform here, with it being cheap on sales because of scepticism around durability and regulation but still pretty expensive on earnings because investors expect operating leverage and monetisation to improve meaningfully if their growth initiatives play out.”

  • Nvidia (NVDA): Upgrade to Buy by Joseph Parrish. The upgrade is driven by NVIDIA’s exceptional execution and a massive order backlog from hyperscalers that secures growth through 2027, with attractive free cash flow margins supporting the valuation.

    “Other megacaps have hundreds of billions in revenue because people need groceries, phones, and energy. Nvidia's value-add was still too abstract to me, and I doubted it. It didn't look doomed, but I couldn't see the reason to buy...But now I don't have those doubts. I think it brings tangible value, and I want to get into that.”

Downgrades

  • Marvell Technology (MRVL): Downgrade Buy to Hold by Juxtaposed Ideas. Despite strong fundamentals in the AI and data center sectors, the analyst believes the stock’s recent rally has pushed the valuation to unsustainable levels with significant downside risk.

    “Despite their structurally robust, profitable growth prospects during the multi-year cloud super cycle, I believe that investors may be better off waiting for a moderate correction nearer to the prior support levels of $110s for an improved margin of safety, with those levels also nearer to my fair value estimates of $112.”

  • Verizon Communications (VZ): Downgrade Buy to Hold by Kenio Fontes. The analyst cites rising long-term threats from Amazon’s satellite investments and modest growth guidance amid a highly competitive telecommunications landscape.

    “At the moment, Verizon has many more reasons to worry about traditional competition than about new players, but I believe that in the next 10 years this can change the game a lot and be a point of disruption."

data.title: Corvus jumps as Goldman Sachs initiates at Buy on lead asset
data.description:
Shares of Corvus Pharmaceuticals (CRVS) spiked on Friday after Goldman Sachs launched its coverage with a Buy recommendation and a $40 target, calling the biotech’s lead candidate, soquelitinib, a pipeline-in-a-product.

Analyst Paul Choi's bullish view comes at a time when shares of the South San Francisco, California-based company have climbed more than 400% over the past 12 months.

However, Choi argued that Corvus (CRVS) shares are attractively positioned, as the stock, at its current valuation, has yet to reflect soquelitinib’s potential against atopic dermatitis and other related autoimmune conditions, as well as peripheral T-cell lymphoma.

The analyst noted that all those programs have catalysts scheduled for the next 12-18 months.

Choi identified soquelitinib as one of the most promising candidates for refractory atopic dermatitis, a condition with a large unmet need for next-gen therapies, according to key opinion leaders.

"While it is still at an early developmental stage, we can envision CRVS leveraging soquelitinib and gradually maturing into large-cap biotech over the long term," the analyst added.

data.title: Cohen & Steers expects 40% compensation ratio and mid-single-digit G&A growth for 2026 amid ETF expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cohen & Steers (CNS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Yesterday, we reported earnings of $0.79 per share as compared to $0.81 sequentially. Revenue for Q1 increased from the prior quarter by 0.3% to $144.3 million." (Interim CFO Michael Donohue)

  • "Ending AUM in Q1 was $93.1 billion, which was up from $90.5 billion at the end of Q4." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "The first quarter's fundamental highlights include net inflows of $497 million a strong one unfunded pipeline of $1.7 billion, characterized by good velocity with continued fundings and new mandates, stable fee rates strong absolute performance and neutral relative performance." (CEO Joseph Harvey)

  • "Our corporate strategy for active ETFs is going very well. Total AUM for our first five ETFs is currently $675 million." (CEO Harvey)

  • "Our nontraded REIT Cohen & Steers Income Opportunities REIT has established a portfolio of 11 properties owned or under contract totaling $650 million in assets and continues to provide investment performance at the top of the real estate peer group with 10.6% annualized returns since inception against the 4.3% peer average." (CEO Harvey)

  • "On a 1-year basis, 86% of our AUM has outperformed its benchmark, while our 3- and 5-year outperformance rates are both above 97%. 95% of our open-end fund AUM is rated 4- or 5-star by Morningstar, which is up from 90% last quarter." (President & Chief Investment Officer John Cheigh)

Outlook

  • "With respect to compensation and benefits, we would expect our compensation ratio to remain at 40% as we experienced in Q1." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "We expect G&A to increase in the mid-single digits for the year as compared to the prior year." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "Lastly, regarding 2026 guidance, we expect our effective tax rate to remain consistent at 25.5% on an as-adjusted basis." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "We announced the conversion of our future of energy open-end fund to an ETF which should occur sometime midyear." (CEO Harvey)

Financial Results

  • "Operating income was $50.7 million during the quarter compared to $52.4 million sequentially. The and our operating margin was 35.1% compared to 36.4% in the prior quarter." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "Our liquidity totaled $343 million at quarter end, which represents a decrease of $60 million versus the prior period." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "Our active ETFs continued their momentum with $224 million of third-party net flows in the quarter." (CEO Harvey)

  • "Sub-advisory experienced $269 million of net outflows in the quarter with $164 million in outflows from Japan." (CEO Harvey)

Q&A

  • John Dunn, Evercore ISI: "You mentioned it's been 2 straight inflow quarters" in advisory and asked whether it was sustainable and driven by new or existing clients; CEO Harvey: "we now have a very strong pipeline, I think, for the third straight quarter at $1.7 billion" and "we were awarded $574 million of new mandates" while describing "good velocity" and saying, "we're optimistic that the institutional advisory channel will continue to perform better and better."

  • John Dunn, Evercore ISI: Asked on active ETFs, including "any cannibalization" and demand pockets; CEO Harvey: "The tone in active ETFs is very good" and "in our business projections for the ETFs, we've factored in some the cannibalization, so to speak" while adding, "I would say I'm very bullish on this vehicle" and noting, "we received our first placement on a major broker-dealer platform."

  • John Dunn, Evercore ISI: Asked whether global real estate interest was rising; CEO Harvey: "We've seen more interest and flows into our global real estate strategy. So I would expect that to continue."

  • Macrae Sykes, Gabelli Funds: Asked what catalysts drive bigger platform shifts into real estate; CIO Cheigh: "it's a combination of investors thinking about the interest rate cycle as well as the fundamental or supply and demand cycle" and "the fundamental inflection is probably the bigger thing that our investors are focused on."

  • Macrae Sykes, Gabelli Funds: Asked how CNS competes with private-credit products given adviser incentives; CEO Harvey: "we think about every morning we would get up is delivering investment performance and managing risk," and an Unknown Executive added, "we've made it very investor-friendly compared with the peer group."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts slightly negative: John Dunn pressed on sustainability and product mechanics, including "more sustainable" advisory flows and "any cannibalization," while Macrae Sykes focused on competitive distribution friction, asking, "how do you sort of compete" with adviser incentives.

  • Management slightly positive: CEO Harvey used confident language on key initiatives, including "I would say I'm very bullish on this vehicle" (ETFs) and "we're optimistic that the institutional advisory channel will continue to perform better and better," while also framing macro uncertainty: "consensus postwar is force stagflation with the key unknowables being how much and for how long."

  • Versus last quarter: In Q4 2025, management highlighted macro rotation with broad confidence, including "we expect economic activity, and market returns to broaden" (CIO Cheigh), while Q1 2026 incorporated a sharper geopolitical overlay, including "the world changed with U.S. military operations in Iran" (CEO Harvey).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "Revenue for Q1 increased from the prior quarter by 0.3% to $144.3 million" (Interim CFO Donohue) compared with Q4 2025: "Revenue for Q4 increased 2% sequentially to $143.8 million." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "Our corporate strategy for active ETFs is going very well. Total AUM for our first five ETFs is currently $675 million" (CEO Harvey) compared with Q4 2025: "We closed the year with 5 ETFs and total AUM of $378 million." (CEO Harvey)

  • "Sub-advisory experienced $269 million of net outflows in the quarter" (CEO Harvey) compared with Q4 2025: "Subadvisory had $30 million of net inflows." (CEO Harvey)

  • "We would expect our compensation ratio to remain at 40%" and "G&A to increase in the mid-single digits" (Interim CFO Donohue) compared with Q4 2025: "we would expect our compensation ratio to remain at 40%" and "projecting [G&A] to be in the mid-single-digit percentage range." (Interim CFO Donohue)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Business activity slowed for a period as investors attempted to calibrate how long the conflict will last and what the short- and long-term ramifications could be for economies, geopolitics and asset allocation." (CEO Harvey)

  • "A key question for CNS REIT short term is how redemption constraints in private wealth vehicles will affect investor appetite for evergreen vehicles generally." (CEO Harvey)

  • "Sub-advisory experienced $269 million of net outflows in the quarter with $164 million in outflows from Japan." (CEO Harvey)

  • "Preferred securities and fixed income classes broadly declined slightly in the quarter as renewed inflation concerns indicate that monetary policy could be tighter for longer." (President & CIO Cheigh)

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized modest sequential declines in profitability alongside higher AUM, positioning Q1 around continued positive net flows and a $1.7 billion pipeline while expanding distribution and ETFs. Leadership repeatedly framed investor behavior through the lens of geopolitical shock and liquidity sensitivity, with CEO Joseph Harvey tying product demand to a preference for “hard assets” and liquidity, highlighting ETF scaling, the planned midyear fund-to-ETF conversion, and continued focus on 2026 expense and tax guidance (40% compensation ratio, mid-single-digit G&A growth, and a 25.5% effective tax rate on an as-adjusted basis).

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Brent oil prices will settle around $80 in the next years – analyst
data.description:
Brent oil prices (CO1:COM) are expected to settle around $80 per barrel over the next two years, according to Warren Pies, co-founder of 3Fourteen Research.

The forecast comes as markets react to the announcement that the Strait of Hormuz is reopening following a period of heightened uncertainty.

In an interview with CNBC, Pies explained that the reopening allows the market to avoid what could have been a far more severe supply disruption.

He noted that the longer the strait remained closed, the higher the risk of elevated long-term oil prices, making the resolution a significant relief for energy markets.

While current prices remain slightly above the $80 level, Pies said the market tends to reach equilibrium quickly.

“I think long term, Brent’s going to settle in around $80 a barrel,” he said, adding that this average would likely hold for the next two years.

Beyond oil, Pies argued that the declining crude prices (CO1:COM), (CL1:COM) strengthen the case for equities (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI).

He described the development as something that “amplifies the equity bull case,” pointing to recent positive momentum in the broader stock market as evidence that a new rally may be underway.

Supporting his bullish outlook, Pies highlighted that earnings have grown 9% year-to-date even as the market experienced a correction. He noted that the price-to-earnings ratio corrected by 18%, a rare occurrence outside of Fed tightening or a recession, which he views as a reset that positions the market for further gains.

Despite the recent volatility, 3Fourteen Research is maintaining its year-end index target of 7,850 for the S&P 500 (SP500). Pies concluded that current conditions, including reset positioning and improving fundamentals, have “the looks of the next leg of the bull market.”

data.title: U.S. stocks rise to new highs as the Strait of Hormuz is declared open
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages jumped on Friday to record highs after Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz open for commercial vessels. President Donald Trump, however, said the naval blockade will remain in effect.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +1.4%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.6%, and the Dow (DJI) was +2.2%.

The Ports and Maritime Organization of the Islamic Republic of Iran said the Strait of Hormuz is now “completely open” for all commercial vessels for the rest of the Lebanon ceasefire timeline. This comes after Israel and Lebanon announced a 10-day ceasefire on Thursday.

Nonetheless, Trump said that the naval blockade on Iranian ports will remain in full force and effect. The reopening of the passageway was one of the conditions of the fragile two-week ceasefire that is currently in effect.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) rapidly decreased to $83, while Brent (CO1:COM) also dropped to $89 per barrel.

In addition, the U.S. dollar (DXY) retreated sharply, -0.3%, erasing its recent Middle East conflict-driven gains as the Strait of Hormuz was declared open to commercial ships by Iran.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 9 basis points lower at 4.23%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 10 basis points to 3.69%.

“The reported reopening of the Strait of Hormuz by Iran is undoubtedly a welcome relief for the global economy. Needless to say, its true impact depends on the durability of this reopening, which will require sustained confidence-building measures from all three warring parties,” said Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz.

“Equally critical is the reaction of ship operators. While their immediate priority is to bring out vessels currently in the region as soon as they can, the real litmus test lies in their willingness to send new ships in. Their return is necessary for restoring the global energy flow and bringing regional production sites fully back online,” El-Erian added.
